D9 HARLEM VOTES™

Meet Your Neighbors

Real Barriers. Real Laws. Real Protections.

Ten Harlem neighbors, and the laws that protect their vote — in plain language, with sources.

Refused at the polls? You can still vote.

Ask for an affidavit ballot — New York’s name for a provisional ballot. It is the same thing. If there is a dispute, you have the right to contact the Election Day Supreme Court Justice for an order to vote that same day, and the poll site must give you that Justice’s contact and location. It is your right.

Your federal right under the Help America Vote Act of 2002 (HAVA), §302 · 52 U.S.C. §21082

Real Barriers

01

Denise's District

Voting Rights Act of 1965 · John R. Lewis Voting Rights Act of New York (2022)
Federal protection weakened (2026) · New York’s still stands

The federal Voting Rights Act of 1965 (52 U.S.C. §10301) long guarded against racial discrimination in voting. Its Section 2 protection against vote dilution was sharply weakened by the U.S. Supreme Court in Louisiana v. Callais (April 29, 2026) — after Shelby County v. Holder (2013) ended preclearance and Brnovich v. DNC (2021) narrowed Section 2. New York’s own John R. Lewis Voting Rights Act (N.Y. Election Law §17-200), enacted in 2022 and upheld by a state appeals court in 2025, still protects against vote dilution, intimidation, deception, and obstruction.

02

Marcus & His ID

The Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act (SAVE Act) · H.R. 22
Passed the U.S. House Feb 11, 2026 · being debated in the Senate · not yet law

The SAVE Act would amend the National Voter Registration Act of 1993 (“Motor Voter,” 52 U.S.C. §20501) to require documentary proof of citizenship to register. If it passes, a standard REAL ID may not count. Documents that would prove citizenship: a U.S. passport, a certified birth certificate, a Certificate of Naturalization, or a New York Enhanced ID (issued only to U.S. citizens).

Real Laws

03

Jeannine's Protection

New York Gender Recognition Act (2021)

New York’s Gender Recognition Act (2021) lets residents update the name and sex designation on a state ID — including an “X” marker — by self-attestation. No court order or doctor’s note is required for the DMV gender marker.

04

Fatou's Decision

U.S. Citizenship · Immigration and Nationality Act

Naturalized citizens have exactly the same right to register and vote as citizens by birth. A Certificate of Naturalization, issued under the Immigration and Nationality Act, is itself proof of U.S. citizenship.

05

Deacon Johnson Educates

John R. Lewis Voting Rights Act of New York (2022)

When federal protections fall short, New York’s John R. Lewis Voting Rights Act (N.Y. Election Law §17-200) protects voters — barring racial vote dilution, intimidation, deception, and obstruction, and requiring certain jurisdictions to get election changes pre-approved.

06

Renée's Trip

New York Early Mail Voter Act (2023)

New York’s Early Mail Voter Act (enacted 2023, effective 2024, upheld in court in 2024) lets any registered voter vote by mail — no excuse needed. Mail it or drop it off by the deadline.

Real Protections

07

Tasha's Move

Affidavit / Provisional Ballot · HAVA §302 · N.Y. Election Law §8-302

Moved, or your name isn’t on the list? You can still vote by affidavit ballot — New York’s provisional ballot (N.Y. Election Law §8-302). This is guaranteed by the federal Help America Vote Act of 2002 (HAVA), Section 302 (52 U.S.C. §21082): if your eligibility is questioned, you must be allowed to cast a provisional/affidavit ballot.

08

Carl Votes

New York Voting-Rights Restoration · Chapter 103, Laws of 2021

In New York, your right to vote is restored automatically when you are released from prison — whether or not you are on parole (Chapter 103 of the Laws of 2021; S.830-B / A.4448-A; signed May 4, 2021). Re-register after you come home.

09

Priya & Preston

New York Voter Pre-Registration · N.Y. Election Law §5-507

New York lets 16- and 17-year-olds pre-register to vote (N.Y. Election Law §5-507). The registration activates automatically when they turn 18.

10

Living in a shelter?

Shelter as Residence · N.Y. Election Law §5-104

You don’t need a lease, a utility bill, or a permanent address. Under New York’s residence rules (N.Y. Election Law §5-104), a shelter — or another place you stay — can be your voting residence.

The laws, in full

  • Help America Vote Act of 2002 (HAVA) — Pub. L. 107-252; §302 (52 U.S.C. §21082): provisional / affidavit ballots.
  • Voting Rights Act of 1965 — 52 U.S.C. §10301; Section 2 weakened by Louisiana v. Callais (2026).
  • National Voter Registration Act of 1993 (“Motor Voter”) — 52 U.S.C. §20501.
  • Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act (SAVE Act) — H.R. 22, 119th Congress; passed the House Feb 2026, under Senate debate, not law.
  • Immigration and Nationality Act — naturalization; Certificate of Naturalization as proof of citizenship.
  • John R. Lewis Voting Rights Act of New York — N.Y. Election Law §17-200 (2022); upheld 2025.
  • New York Gender Recognition Act — 2021.
  • New York Early Mail Voter Act — 2023 (effective 2024).
  • New York voting-rights restoration on release — Chapter 103, Laws of 2021.
  • New York voter pre-registration (16 & 17) — N.Y. Election Law §5-507.
  • New York affidavit ballot — N.Y. Election Law §8-302.
  • New York residence rules — N.Y. Election Law §5-104.

This guide is general information, not legal advice. Confirm deadlines and details at vote.nyc or with the NYC Board of Elections. Statute citations are provided for reference; verify exact subsections for any official use.

Established in 2024, a nonpartisan civic education coalition comprised of members of historically Black fraternities and sororities serving Harlem, New York.
